When leaders are not aware of the economic terminology called ... multiplier effects ..the the real problem starts ...in ordinary saying ...we call it Chain reactions. 
When an important essential commodity increases in price ... lots and lots of things will follow suit as they are affected too.

author: @Scirwode
text:
Good, it's high time all subsidies are removed and people start to live a cost effective lifestyle. Just because you can afford a Honda Civic doesn't mean you can afford the maintenance i.e. tyres, fuel, service, parts etc. It would also mean less cars on the road, hence less wastage in terms of energy and time.

What the government needs to do now is to improve the last mile connection, funneling people to take public transportation and improving side walks and making KL a walkable city. The fact that our insurance premiums for cars is miles cheaper compared to the UK or even the EU makes car ownership a given.

It also means more people will start to cook at home, and only going out for special occasions, which makes it more meaningful. Enough is enough, we need to stop being a welfare state or it might become to late. You only have to look at the UK and see how far it has fallen.

author: @francissia4660
text:
各位同胞、兄弟姐妹们：

​现在的政局确实令人感到焦虑和不安。但在情绪波动之时，我们更需要冷静思考：华社在马来西亚的政治出路究竟在哪里？

​我们要明白几个残酷的现实：

​政治席位决定话语权： 华裔是少数群体。如果代表华裔的主要政党退出团结政府，选择孤军奋战，最终的结果很可能是永远沦为反对党。当内阁里没有华裔代表，谁来在决策圈内为我们的教育、经济和文化发声？

​权力真空的代价： 如果华裔退出主流政治核心，留下的权力真空必然会被更保守的势力（如PAS等）填补。一旦内阁彻底“单色化”，政策的走向将难以回弹，华社的处境将更加艰难。

​国家稳定的基石： 无论是砂拉越还是沙巴，大家都在看西马的局势。如果因为我们的内部分裂而引发政局动荡，不仅外资会流失，东马的稳定也会受影响。经济搞不好，受苦的是每一个人。

​我们的呼吁：

​大局为重： 政治不是完美的，但必须是务实的。留在体制内争取利益，远比在体制外呐喊更有力。

​拒绝情绪化分化： 不要因为一时的不满而自乱阵脚。失去团结，受惠的只会是那些极端势力。

​为了下一代： 想想我们的未来五年，甚至下一代。我们需要一个稳定、多元、包容的政府，而不是一个走向极端、将华裔边缘化的国家。

​同胞们，请醒觉！我们要的是团结的力量，而不是分裂的自由。只有留在主流，我们才有机会守护家园。

text: Unrelated but I know economists have been calling for abolishment of subsidies in Malaysia so that government can better use the "fallout" to fund other projects but i beg to differ. The more i compare Malaysia to other countries especially western countries that are against subsidies i am beginning to see we might be doing the right thing not only for the people but also for the economy. The reason i say that is compare them or even some developing Asian countries, when basic necessities like oil, rice and cooking oil price are capped prices are kept relatively low here, that put more money into the packet of the people which encourage spending. This applies not only to the people but businesses as well in the form of lower cost of operations. Take for example in a country where you are already at your spending limit paying home mortgage, utility bills, car installment, groceries etc you will not eat out or buy stuffs as frequent which dampen businesses and subsequently the economy. Having said that, wages do pay a big part in all of this but i do feel what the government loose in terms of subsidies the people will gain and at the end of the day it is the purpose of a government

author: @skyjo
text:
Not telling the full story is a selfish sensationalization of the story for clicks and drama.

Malaysia is a net exporter of energy, with high income from high quality fuel being exported at high market prices (oil doubling in price also means our income from oil exports doubles)... that directly channels to the budget for local fuel subsidies, specifically ron95.

Will diesel that runs most trucks affect the pricing of goods? Yes. But transportation is not the bulk of the cost of goods and therefore certainly not a 1:1 increase in fuel vs prices of goods.

Please don't cause unnecessary panic which has real affects on the economy and therefore everyone.

Unemployment at a record low of 2.9% has a much bigger (positive) impact to everyone than oil doubling in price.

Tell a balanced story and encourage people... not scare for clicks. Tsk tsk

author: @banana_ackchyual
text:
The current system of fuel subsidy couldn't end soon enough. Malaysians' addiction to abnormally cheap fuel at the cost of the county's financial future is frankly disgusting. People are extremely wasteful with their car usage because they take it for granted that ron95 is extremely cheap. How many times have you seen people leave their empty car running with the AC on and parked in an inconsiderate way while they go buy a few things from a kedai runcit? How many times have you seen people eating McD in their cars parked illegally on the side of the road engine and AC running? Someone has to foot the bill for their wasteful behavior.

Subsidy either has to apply to everyone for it to be fair or to those who truly need it to be fair. The current system simply placates the majority while screwing over the minority. I would be all for monthly cash payouts tied to your IC and that can be used to purchase ALL fuel types at market price or subsidized fuel only being accessible to the very poor and industries that deliver value to society (f&b, logistics, etc.).

I hope the current system shatters to pieces. The current conflict in Iran could be the perfect straw that breaks the camel's back if the current administration plays its cards right. I'd rather be earning enough money to be able to buy as much fuel as I want at market rate then keep "enjoying" ron95 at 1.99/l (which I don't because I exclusively pump ron97 because I care about my engine) but continue to earn peanut salary. The subsidy money could be put to better use elsewhere like investing in better infrastructure or expanding public transport etc.

author: @derrickk7081
text:
While i used to be in this camp, you need to understand that our oil is jet grade and not meant to power our cars. The prices of our oil while it is priced higher, the margin between that of the imported oil for our cars contracts further with everything that is happening. From the oil that the tankers use to ship those oil back here and ours to export, down to the tankers/trucks that distribute the oil, all of this is added to the cost within that margin. It’s a great feat of management so far of how the current government is shouldering this by losing 4 billion a month while leadership is still keeping a cool head. Let that sink in before we start bickering at them. 

While i do hope they don’t remove it, i certainly appreciate the effort and measures they’re doing to keep it there. And if the people really want to help to stretch that subsidy that is currently sustaining our lifestyle instead of all talk, we should help by championing WFH again at the very least especially if your work doesn’t really need you to be in the office and can be managed with zoom for now. It may be a hit for the offices again in the interim, but its impact is huge that’ll stretch our fuel reserves further which would help everyone else. But if no one is doing their part in that, i do hope the government attempts to temporarily enforce WFH for a few months before they remove the subsidy as a last resort. And after we’re out of the woods on this crisis, they’d invest heavily on public transport and infrastructure.

text: My workplace is 50km from where I lived so that's a 100km daily travel. I work 26 days a month so that's 2,600km monthly travel. I drive a 2019 Proton Saga & based on google search the fuel consumption is about 7.5L - 8.5L/100km for urban city driving (make it 8L/100km for easy calculation). So for a 2,600km monthly travel, it'll consume about 208L/month. I've just pumped my car on 20/4 & has only 47L left in my subsidy. I pumped every 4 days & my tank can hold about 30L of fuel. author: @RyuKyu316
text:
​ @andrewm3961  Definitely agree. I sweat super easily and definitely will not walk if there is no proper infrastructure. But I've tried walking in car-free zones in Melaka during the evening and night, and imo it's good no need to find parking and can go anywhere I want. Sadly, it's only viable if the day is not hot. 

Imo walkable cities are much easier to achieve in countries that have moderate weather, like temps around 15-25 degrees. No need for covered walkways, just build more connections for the walkways. In Malaysia, the problem is whether there are walkways that are connected; no one will choose to walk because it's so hot or raining outside. 

The solution I can think of is to make walking more convenient and comfortable, as most people will always choose the path of least resistance. For example, most people choose to drive because it's more comfortable and the only downside is jam and parking. At least when they arrive at their destination, they are not hot and sweaty or drenched in rain. Therefore, if we do changes like covered walkways and maybe find ways to cool them like trees or fans or even air conditioning idk, more people will be willing to walk.

Xyh kira kilometer bro. Ko kira je purata berapa rm ko pakai sebulan sblm ni. Bpe ko peruntukkan utk minyak saja. Bg yg pkai motor pi keje mmg x terkesan tp yg pkai kete tu akn rasa bro. 

Bygkn tmpt keje ko tu 25km. Jalan jem. Lazim 25km tu sekali jalan dlm rm15. Pegi balik dh rm30. Klau sebulan keje tolak sabtu ahad rm30x20 = rm600 bro. Pegi keje saja dh nk smpai limit 300liter tu. Belum sentuh bab jalan2, beli brg & balik kg bro..

author: @mohamadarifinaliarifin2903
text:
Untuk pengguna biasa, 4 hingga 5 kali isi sebulan sudah cukup...sebab 300 liter boleh isi dalam 7 kali isi sebulan. kalau yg jarak jauh hari hari , 2, 3 hari sekali, sebulan boleh capai  8 kali atau 9 kali, lebih sikit dari quota. Tapi boleh guna quota isteri kot....tapi mesti ada lesen...
Yg jadi masalah warga asing berapa pulak harga, kalau dulu kena isi ron 97, 3.20 rm, sekarang ? harga ron 95 tanpa subsidi dengarnya 2.60. Jika mereka bolih beli yg ni, dah untung pulak , 60 sen setiap liter. 40 liter, satu tangki penuh mereka dapat durian runtuh, 24 rm. Sebulan jika lima kali, 120 rm. Sekarang sistem baru untungkan siapa? Rakyat macam macam syarat, hanya dapat 6 sen. Warga asing jika beli 2.60 untung 60 sen tak perlu pening pening.....dengan itu makin sesak dengan warga asing lah negara ini. Kera disusukan, anak kelaparan...jika 2.60 sen untuk mereka. 
Jangan pulak ada yg akan kata isilah yg 2.60 tu jika nak untung....itu memang bodoh tak ada akai.

text:
Kalau dicongak secara simple matematik la, ambil contoh keeeta yg fuel consumtion dia 7 liter/100km..mungkin myvi kot dan jarak perjalanan pi kerja sehala 70km. Mcm seremban - sg buluh dan kerja 20nm hari sebulan :

70x2x20 =2800km sebulan.
100km - 7 ltr
2800km - 7 x 2800 ÷ 100 = 196 liter sebulan.
Kalau guna masa hari minggu keluar jalan² 4 kali sebulan katalah 100km seminggu, sebulan 28 liter. Penggunaan baru 226 liter. 
Kalau sulam sulam pulak dgn motor lagi la lebih dr cukup.

text:
macamana pegi seremban sg buloh dpt 70km? sbb bila tgk kat map dari sendayan 90km++ ke hospital sg buloh. lgpun tak boleh kira 20 hari bekerja sebulan. sbb sebulan ada 30/31 hari kecuali februari. dan tak semua bulan mula dgn hari sama. dan bukan semua org guna myvi. kena ambil kira kereta yang fuel consumption tak sama dgn kereta perodua.

contoh dgn preve.
avrg fuel consumption: 10km/l (mixed highway n town)
avrg daily commute sendayan-hosp sg buloh: 90km x 2= 180km
monthly: 180km x 22 ( avrg 30days/mnth minus 8 days weekend) = 3960km
monthly usage: 396 liters. (10km/l)

contoh dgn axia
avrg fuel consumption: 18km/l (mixed highway n town)
avrg daily commute sendayan-hosp sg buloh: 90km x 2= 180km
monthly: 180km x 22 ( avrg 30days/mnth minus 8 days weekend) = 3960km
monthly usage: 220 liters. (18km/l)

lain kereta lain fuel consumption.

text:
Satu lagi kan, 200km sehari = 26 hari bekerja = 5200km sebulan. Kalau kereta lama mungkin guna dalam 11.1km/L maksudnya perlu 468L. Harga subsidi sekarang RM2.05. Sebulan bayar RM959.40 untuk petrol pergi kerja saja. Logik ke?

author: @pengangurterhormat
text:
Buat sifir tunjuk formula ofcos lah orang susah nak faham. Ini cara kira yg paling senang…..
1. Mula mula tengok tangki minyak kereta anda kalau isi penuh berapa litre boleh isi.
2. Contoh kalau isi penuh 50 litre.
3. Ini bermakna anda boleh minyak penuh sebanyak 6 kali sebulan .
4. So kalau anda sekarang isi minyak penuh 2 kali seminngu bermakana kuota anda akan habis dalam masa 3 minggu.
5. Kebaikan disini ialah kerajaan mengalakan kita berjimat. 
6. Bagi yg kereta tangki besar kuota cepat habislah sebab sekali isi penuh 70 litre. 4 kali isi penuh dah 280 litre.
7. So sekarang jom g tengok kereta masing masing kalau isi penuh berapa litre 🤣lu kira lah sendiri❤
7. Orang kerja dalam bandar biasanya isi penuh seminggu sekali purata❤

text: I don’t understand the logic behind imposing different tax rates on foreigners for things like medical services, petrol, and more. The majority of foreigners in Malaysia are low-income workers and labourers why burden them further with extra costs? If one day their home countries decide to stop sending their citizens to work here, how will Malaysia manage? The economy depends heavily on these workers, and such policies risk creating unnecessary tension.

author: @ghrbaa6727
text: There is no different “tax rates”. What exists is preferential subsidy for our own people. Because why should we pay for foreigners in the first place who already benefit from the artificially low prices of goods and services from government subsidy. Stop acting as if the benefits of subsidy do not extend to foreigners in Malaysia. In case you missed the news we are trying to cut our dependence on low cost workers as it has caused Malaysian wages to be disproportionately low and stem our wage growth. 10% of the labour force being foreigners is not something we are proud of. And no, you are wrong. The home countries of those workers basically beg the government so that they can send their workers here. Even Saudi Arabia has started labour force revolution, and changes in employment there and countries like UAE for locals and automation will actually put pressure on countries who export their workers. What I’m saying is, there will always be those who we can find to work here if we want to.

text: Volatility in global oil prices has made it challenging for Malaysia's government to carry out a plan to cut petrol subsidies. Each year, Malaysia spends billions of dollars to keep retail pump prices below market price. Despite concerns over inflation, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim has promised to remove the blanket subsidy that will help save the government some US$8 billion. Experts have called for the government to rationalise subsidies and move towards a more targeted one to help those in need. Melissa Goh reports from Kuala Lumpur.

author: @Yangmuliasharifah
text:
Dua kapal. Sama-sama bawa minyak. Sama-sama nak lalu selat yang sama. Tapi satu lepas, satu lagi masih terkandas sejak sebulan lepas.

Kapal Malaysia dah lalu. Kapal Indonesia masih tunggu.

Ini bukan cerita pasal kapal. Ini cerita pasal dua negara yang buat pilihan berbeza, dan sekarang sedang hidup dengan akibat pilihan tu.

Pergi balik tiga tahun. Julai 2023. Pasukan maritim Indonesia tangkap sebuah kapal tangki gergasi Iran bernama MT Arman 114 di Laut Natuna Utara. Kapal tu buat pemindahan minyak secara haram di tengah laut. Buang sisa. Cemar perairan. Indonesia rampas kapal, rampas minyak, bawa ke mahkamah.

Iran minta kapal tu dikembalikan.

Indonesia kata tidak.

Iran minta berunding.

Indonesia kata biar mahkamah yang putuskan.

Awal 2026, Indonesia umumkan kapal Iran bernilai RM340 juta itu akan dilelong. Tidak ada siapa yang beli dalam lelongan pertama. Lelongan kedua sedang berjalan.

Iran diam. Tapi Iran ingat.

Kemudian perang bermula 28 Februari. Khamenei meninggal dalam serangan pertama. Pemimpin baru Iran lebih keras, lebih berani, dan mempunyai senarai nama negara yang mereka percaya dan tidak percaya.

Indonesia tidak ada dalam senarai yang dipercayai.

Sekarang dua kapal Pertamina terkandas di Teluk Arab. Pertamina Pride membawa dua juta barel minyak untuk keperluan tenaga dalam negeri Indonesia. Setiap hari ia tidak bergerak, Indonesia makin hampir ke garisan bahaya. Simpanan minyak Indonesia tinggal 21 hari. Kapal yang sepatutnya isi semula simpanan tu masih berlabuh menunggu kebenaran yang belum tiba.

Menteri Tenaga Bahlil ditanya wartawan setiap hari. Jawapan dia sama setiap kali. Masih negosiasi. Masih negosiasi. Masih negosiasi.

Sementara itu di Kuala Lumpur, PM Anwar sudah hubungi pihak Iran terus. Hubungi Egypt. Hubungi Turkey. Malaysia tidak sokong serangan, tidak bergabung dengan mana-mana pakatan yang Iran anggap musuh, dan rekod tu bercakap sendiri. Kapal Malaysia dapat kebenaran lalu.

Bila Anwar terbang ke Jakarta semalam jumpa Prabowo, di media sosial Indonesia satu soalan mula viral dalam kalangan rakyat mereka sendiri.

Kenapa Malaysia boleh, kita tidak?

Prabowo tidak ada jawapan yang mudah untuk soalan tu. Kerana jawapan sebenarnya bukan pasal hari ini. Ia pasal semua keputusan yang Indonesia buat dalam tiga tahun lepas, satu demi satu, yang secara perlahan mengikis kepercayaan Iran terhadap mereka.

Penulisan ini berdasarkan laporan yang tersedia. Gambaran penuh mungkin berbeza apabila lebih banyak maklumat tersedia.

Selat Hormuz bukan sekadar laluan kapal. Ia adalah ujian. Ujian kepada setiap negara sama ada mereka jaga hubungan baik ketika senang supaya ada modal ketika susah.

Malaysia lulus ujian tu. Indonesia masih ambil semula.

Dua negara serumpun. Bahasa hampir sama. Agama sama. Sejarah hampir sama. Tapi di selat yang sama, nasib dua negara ni hari ini berbeza sepenuhnya.

Siapa yang ada di sini faham gambaran ni lebih awal dari kebanyakan orang. Esok pagi ada sudut lain dalam cerita ini yang ramai orang terlepas pandang, sudut yang tunjukkan apa lagi yang Malaysia buat berbeza dari negara-negara lain dalam menghadapi krisis ini.

Sumber: Kompas, CNN Indonesia, Detik, Dunia Energi, Harapan Daily, Economix

author: @return041
text:
Everybody's shouting "Tax the T20!" like it's some magic cure. Let’s look at our actual tax system (out of a ~17 million workforce):

• Only about 15% of the workforce (~2.5 million people) make enough money to cross the minimum threshold and pay ANY income tax. This tiny group pays 100% of the nation's personal income tax bill.
• Out of that bill, the government's "T20" demographic tanks 85% of it. The upper edge of the "M40" pays the remaining 15%.
• The rest of the M40, and the entire B40, pay zero.

The government throws around the label "T20" to make them sound like elite tycoons. In reality, most of this group are just hardworking professionals with a nice salary on paper, but who are still asset-poor and struggling to buy a decent house in a highly inflated market.

And here is the dark reality. You think going after the ultra-rich business owners and corrupt politicians will save us? You tax the billionaires and the cronies, they don't pay it out of their own pockets. They just pass the cost right back down to us. They hike up prices, freeze our wages, and fire the middle class to protect their profit margins.

The game is rigged. 🤷‍♂

author: @sharonwong8280
text: Malaysia has an entrenched subsidy culture.  What it is based on (aside from the legitimate social contract of helping and lessening the burden of the population), lies a political strategy.  To me, much of our very generous but unsustainable subsidies, is  to assuage the B40 population, largely made up of poor Malays in exchange for their votes.  This borders on the illegitimate especially as the government overtly creates easy pathways for Malays/Bumiputras to jump queues and have special privileges in terms of education/university places; govt. projects; discounts on purchasing homes.  I would like to call this a "subsidy" - that is, a race-based subsidy that eases their burden that is not open to any others EXCEPT the Bumiputras.  This is painfully short-sighted that ultimately, handicaps the very people that needs to be lifted up.  I love Malaysia.  It troubles me that as a country, we are forever stuck with a majority population of Malays who are used to this steady diet of handouts and privileges.  They deserve better.

A British accent has a hypnotic and spell bounding effect, as in the narration of this presentation. The effect here is to suggest lessening subsidies. 
 Neocolonies exist for clear purposes, and Malaysia is not immune from such purposes. 
Being not part of a self sufficient and regulating civilization, removing subsidies without an iron clad alternative is all about benefitting investors in the various privatization schemes that would be hatched to deal with a post subsidized event: and because our research, educational, training, and social relational   capacities  have been sabotaged to not be of much use,  the post-subsidized business will draw on external expertise that will carry  a hefty price tag. 
Politicians in the Third World exist to  serve in  the profits of  neocolonial interests of the dominant powers in indigenous local dress and form to avoid  international litigation. OUR politicians and businessmen are tailored to serve these interests. 
Avoiding subsidies implies having appropriate  levels of real  local research, expertise , and social cooperation essential  to translate research into viable, community serving affordable  solutions.
My guess  is that whatever  decisions  are made on subsidies will not disturb the status quo of dominant powers in ensuring that raw materials, resources and cheap manufacturing are available for profit in the Third World, and subsidies are there to ensure that money is not diverted to provide affordable solutions using available (and NOT non available) local ingenuity and cooperation. 16:07

author: @HeresMyView
text:
Malaysia is an Asian country that takes care of its citizens. The subsidies are to ensure its people can have a decent living standard, roof over their head and a decent education for the economic development of Malaysia. Without subsidies, many people may be facing hunger, poverty, health problem, homelessness and social issues. So, it is a balancing act to provide social network support and economic development. No political stability and economic development & growth can occur when there are social instability, high cost of living, high inflation and social unrest are constantly hurting the population.

In US, a Capitalism system has driven a very wide inequality gap in income and wealth. The rich get filthy rich, and the poor get poorer leading to many social and economic problems - homelessness, dirty unsanitary hazardous roads, street defecation, drug addictions, violence, rising crimes, gang violence and mental health. author: @rahimmajid647
text:
That's the two edged sword problem of subsidies... Subsidies are good for our social welfare, to keep control of the price of our basic neccesities. However, one of the negative effect of subsidies is that it also keeps our basic wage low. What drives basic wages upwards? Basically, inflation and cost of living. As subsidies help keep the price of our basic neccesities low, it will also reduce the pressure to increase our basic wages. 
Don't take me wrong here.... I do not advocate for the abolishment of subsidies as that is just cruel. But we do need to find a better balance here.

author: @timhean
text:
@captainmee3469 subsidies for all, or subsidies for none. I do agree lower class needs a bit of a lift to help them get them to where we want our middle-middle higher income economy which is what our government wants to be by 2030. With how inflation is eating away us, and how our Ringgit hasn’t been strengthening until last quarter, Malaysia has a bleak future till we remove unnecessary stress in the economy, but subsidies are still needed for the everyday Malaysian (B40, M40) to be on at least a perceived level playing field with the U20. That said, I fully support removing Bumi subsidies because it doesn’t make sense that everyone of us are “Malaysians” but some Malaysians are better than others. Unless you are in financial need, your loans and house purchasing rights should be on a level playing field, but that’s too political of an opinion apparently. 

Also Philippines is not a good example of an economic model which we should look at, because it’s an authoritarian state farcading as a democracy. Capitalist conglomerates run the entire nation and I wouldn’t want to see that in Malaysia. I say this coming from an upper middle lower income Malaysian living in Australia earning a decent salary, and I date a very Filipino girl and she can only have the lifestyle they have because of the rampant corruption in their country.

author: @trafficmonsoonacademy562
text:
No chance.

Wanna know why?

Because Singaporeans pumping petrol in JB isn’t just about cheaper fuel — every trip brings business to Malaysia. They don’t only buy petrol; they eat at local restaurants, shop at malls, service their cars, and spend on daily goods. 

This cash flow supports local jobs and keeps many Johor businesses thriving.

Of course, subsidies are meant for Malaysians, and it’s fair to protect that.

But instead of seeing Singaporeans as ‘taking away,’ it can be seen as them contributing back through their spending. 

A balanced system that protects subsidies and keeps Singaporeans coming will actually strengthen Malaysia’s economy in the long run.

SG is 7 times more wealthier than the whole of Malaysia...

BUT if there is a financial crash, global trade stops, WW3, any black swan events....

SG have no one else for support except M'sia. Thats the truth.

Because Msia can self sustain. SG cannot.

The term is call, "Self Sufficiency Ratio" - Msia is ard 80%, SG less than 10%

text:
Rather than dealing with the difficult problem of determining who are in the T15, it is simpler and likely more effective to give a flat monthly fuel subsidy to every adult citizen and half the amount to all child citizen.

The real T15 with their larger vehicles, whether privately owned or via companies will NO longer be able to enjoy unlimited subsidies like they do today.
